rulings.Case Title: GMG JANITORIAL, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SQAN FRANCISCO , A CHARTERED
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, October 16, 2013, Line 3, RESPONDENT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion For Judgment. The court grants the motion for judgment. For purposes of the writ, GMG concedes GMG's liability for violating the Health Care Security Ordinance ("HCSO"). The only issue is the propriety of the $1,339,028.39 corrective action. Substantial evidence in the record supports the finding of the corrective action in this amount. Respondent is entitled to judgment as a matter of law. Reg. 9.1 states that respondent "may order any other actions it deems necessary to correct the violation(s) committed. Given that broad discretion, it cannot be said that Respondent's decision was arbitrary or unreasonable under Yamaha Corp. v. State Bd. of Equalization (1998) 19 Cal. 4th 1, 11. The Court will sign the proposed Judgment Denying Writ of Mandate submitted with the moving papers. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: ST. LUCIA FREE PRESS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Petition To Quash Invalid Subpoena
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 16, 2013, Line 1: Petitioner ST. LUCIA FREE PRESS' Petition To Quash Invalid Subpoena Pro Tem Judge David May, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion Granted. The subpoena for production of business records in S.F. Superior Ct. No. CPF-13-513220 and High Ct. of Justice, Queen¿s Bench Division, Strand, London, U.K. No. HQ13D04242 is hereby Quashed. This case is controlled by Krinsky v. Doe (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 1154, 1172 and CCP §1987.2(c) which hold that plaintiff must plead and provide evidence establishing a prima facie case in order to obtain discovery of the identity of a proposed defendant. ¿We will refrain from ruling on the adequacy of a cause of action that was never pleaded.¿ (Krinsky, supra at p.1173.) Plaintiff has never pled a cause of action, for libel or any other cause. Reasonable attorney fees of $1,800 ($300 per hour for 6 hours) plus costs of $510 are awarded to Petitioner pursuant to CCP §1987.2(c). Petitioner¿s attorney has failed to support her request for attorney fees with proof of the reasonable value of her services (she has been practicing less than 6 years), the amount of time spent, or the reasonableness thereof. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidedwardmay@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SHIRLEY CAMPBELL et al VS. SONORA SPRINGS et al*****CASE TRANSFERRED TO TUOLUMNE COUNTY*****
rulings.Calendar Matter: (Amended) Notice Of Motion For Change Of Venue
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, October 16, 2013, Line 11, DEFENDANTs SONORA SPRINGS INVESTORS, LLC and MOUNTAIN SPRINGS DEVELOPMENT LLC's (Amended) Motion For Change Of Venue. Defendants' motion to transfer venue to Tuolumne County is granted. Defendants' motion is timely as it was filed within a reasonable time after the answer was filed. Defendants have met their burden of showing that the convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice would be promoted by the transfer of venue. (CCP §397(c).) If they have not done so already, Defendants are ordered to post the clerk's costs and fees for transferring the action to Tuolumne County pursuant to CCP §399(a). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL VS. BANK OF THE WEST, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Its Request For Production Of Documents And For Award Of Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 16, 2013, Line 4: Defendant BANK OF THE WEST'S Motion To Compel Responses To Its Request For Production Of Documents And For Award Of Monetary Sanctions Part 1 of 2: Pro Tem Judge David May, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted in part. Plaintiff shall provide further responses, without objection, to the Requests from the Request for Production, Set One, as identified and limited below, within 15 days of notice of entry of this order, and shall produce any further documents in plaintiff¿s (and her attorney¿s) possession or control, but not already produced, at the office of counsel for Bank of the West (650 California St., 20th Flr., San Francisco CA) at 10:00 a.m., 5 court days thereafter. For Request nos. 1, 5, 6: Limited to the period 10/01/2011 to 8/31/12. For Request nos. 5, 6, the terms ¿refer to, or RELATE TO¿ are stricken. For Request nos. 7, 16: Without limitation. For Request nos. 25-28: Limited to the period August 1, 2012 to present. Further, for Request no. 26, limited to job applications, personnel policies, employee handbooks, benefit plans, job evaluations, pay stubs and job descriptions. Further, for Request no. 27, limited to benefit plans, pay stubs, job descriptions, contracts and description of work performed. Further, for Request no. 28, limited to claims for health care or disability benefits. For Request nos. 26 ¿ 28, the terms ¿refer to, or RELATE TO¿ are stricken. Continued to Part 2 of 2. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL VS. BANK OF THE WEST, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Deft'S Further Responses To Form Interrogatories - Employment Law, Special Interrogatories And Award Of Monetary Sanction Against Deft And Its Counsel Littler Mendelson In The Amouht Of $2,665
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 16, 2013, Line 4: Plaintiff NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL'S Motion To Compel Defefendant's Further Responses To Form Interrogatories - Employment Law, Special Interrogatories And Award Of Monetary Sanction Against Defendant And Its Counsel Littler Mendelson In The Amouht Of $2,665 Pro Tem Judge David May, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant¿s general objections and specific objections violated CCP §2030.300(a)(3) because they are too general and are sanctionable ¿boilerplate¿ per Korea Data Systems Co. Ltd. v. Sup. Ct. (Amazing Technologies Corp.) (1997) 51 CA4th, 1513, 1516. Defendant shall answer Special Interrogatories 1-6, inclusive, without objection within 15 days of notice of entry of this order. With respect to contact information requested in Form Interrogatories (Employment) 207.2 and 217.1, the motion is Denied. The witnesses are current or former officers, managers, or employees who are percipient witnesses and whose ¿act[s] or failure to act in connection with the matter . . . may bind the corporation, be imputed to it, or constitute an admission of the corporation for purposes of establishing liability.¿ (Triple A Machine Shop, Inc. v. State of California (1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 131, 140.) Accordingly, they may not be contacted by plaintiff¿s counsel independently by virtue of RPC 2-100(B)(2). plaintiff can take their depositions upon notice to defendant¿s counsel. Hence, their privacy rights outweigh plaintiff¿s interest in their contact information. Life Technologies Corp. v. Superior Court (Timothy H. Joyce) (2011) 197 Cal.App.4th 640, 655. If plaintiff does not produce former employees or officers for deposition upon notice, then plaintiff is entitled to their contact information, subject to an appropriately drafted confidentiality protective order or agreement. Motion for sanctions is Denied. No sanctions are awarded to either party. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidedwardmay@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

Motion for Sanctions is denied. No sanctions are awarded to either party. rulings.Case Title: LAUREL LEARNING CENTER, DBA THE LAUREL SCHOOL VS. DEBRA SCHOENBERG et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Responses To Req. For Production Of Documents, Set One; Form Interrogatories, Set One; Req. For Admissions, Set One; And Special Interrogatories, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 16, 2013, Line 5: Defendant DEBRA SCHOENBERG'S Motion To Compel Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One; Form Interrogatories, Set One; Requests For Admissions, Set One; And Special Interrogatories, Set One Part 1 of 2: Pro Tem Judge David May, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted in part, and denied in part. Denied as to Request for Production nos. 1-6 & 8. No notice was given to the third party. (Calif. Const. Art. I, Sec. 1; CCP § 1985.3; Life Technologies Corp. v. Sup.Ct. (2011) 197 Cal.App.4th 640, 655 and Pioneer Electronics (USA), Inc. v. SupCt. (2007) 40 Cal.4th 360.) School records are expressly protected as ¿personal records¿ by CCP §1985.3, and the facts that the third party is a child and the nature of the school heighten the privacy concerns. Granted as to Form Interrogatory nos. 3.7. 17.1. For no. 17.1, the answer shall be complete and as straightforward as the information reasonably permits; reference to another set of responses is inadequate. Denied, as to Form Interrogatory nos. 4.1, 8.1, 8.2, 8.7, 8.8, 9.1, 12.1-12.7, 14.1-14.2, 15.1, 16.1-16.10. No. 4.1 is ambiguous since the definition of INCIDENT could refer to the breach of contract claim of the complaint or the alleged assault of the cross-complaint. Further, as to the cross-complaint there is insufficient description of the INCIDENT. Nos. 8.1, 8.2, 8.7, 8.8, 9.1, 12.1-12.7, 14.1-14.2 are designed to be addressed to a plaintiff in a personal injury claim, not a contract claim (see Form Interrogatories 50.1-50.6). The interrogatory is ambiguous since the definition of INCIDENT could refer to the breach of contract claim of the complaint or the alleged assault of the cross-complaint. Further, as to the cross-complaint there is insufficient description of the INCIDENT. Denied as to Request for Admission nos. 1-9. For nos. 6-9, no notice was given to the third party. (See supra.) School records are expressly protected as ¿personal records¿ by CCP §1985.3, and the facts that the third party is a child and the nature of the school heighten the privacy concerns. Denied as to Special Interrogatory nos. 1-14. For nos. 2-5, 7 ¿ 9, the term ¿incident¿ is not sufficiently full and complete in and of itself. No. 6 is defective in that it assumes an answer to Special Interrogatory No. 5 (and there were two Special Interrogatory No. 5's) that did not exist. Nos. 10-14 assume an answer to Special Interrogatory No. 9 that did not exist. Continued to Part 2 of 2. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JUSTIN MOORE VS. RICHARD BURDEN TEED
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena To Third Party Axcient
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 16, 2013, Line 6: Plaintiff JUSTIN MOORE'S Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena To Third Party Axcient Pro Tem Judge David May, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion is granted. The Deposition Subpoena For Production of Business Records directed to First Republic Bank dated Aug. 26, 2013 is hereby Quashed. Plaintiff's ability to pay for increased costs not anticipated at time of purchase of property is not at issue in any cause of action. Allegation in 2nd Cause of Action (para. 82) that plaintiff was induced by defendant(s) into "spending far more for the Property than he could afford," is surplusage, not relevant to any material fact required to establish a cause of action, and thus does not support invasion of plaintiff's privacy. (Art. I, Sec. 1, Calif. Const.; Fortunato v. Sup. Ct. (Ingrassia) (2003) 114 Cal.App.4th 475, 480-1.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidedwardmay@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
